definition: A decision tree is a supervised model that recursively partitions feature space with axis-aligned binary splits, routing each sample from root to leaf; leaves predict a class majority (classification) or mean target (regression). CART builds trees greedily by minimizing impurity (Gini or entropy) at each split.
layman: A decision tree is a flowchart of yes/no questions about your data — like "Is income above $50k?" then "Is credit score below 650?" — until you reach a final answer box that gives the predicted class or number.
content:
## Decision trees and CART

A **decision tree** learns a hierarchy of **binary splits** on input features. Each internal **node** asks a question about one feature; each branch follows the answer; each **leaf** holds a prediction.

**CART** (Classification And Regression Trees) is the standard algorithm family used in scikit-learn and most libraries:

- **Classification**: leaf prediction = **majority class** among training samples that reach that leaf.
- **Regression**: leaf prediction = **mean** (or median in some variants) of the target among samples in the leaf.

Trees are built **top-down**, **recursively**, and **greedily**: at each node, CART searches all features and candidate split thresholds, picks the split that most reduces **impurity**, then repeats on child nodes until a stopping rule fires.

### How a split is chosen

For a node containing training samples S, CART evaluates splits of the form:

**"Is feature j ≤ threshold t?"**

This partitions S into left child L and right child R. The algorithm picks (j, t) that maximizes **impurity reduction** (equivalently, maximizes **information gain** when using entropy).

Common **impurity** measures for classification (canonical definitions in this topic):

| Measure | Formula (K classes, proportions p_k) | Range |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Gini impurity** | G = 1 − Σ_k p_k² | [0, 1−1/K] |
| **Entropy** | H = −Σ_k p_k log₂ p_k | [0, log₂ K] |

**Weighted impurity after split**:

I_after = (|L|/|S|) · I(L) + (|R|/|S|) · I(R)

**Gain** = I(S) − I_after — larger gain means a more informative split.

Gini and entropy usually rank splits similarly; Gini is slightly cheaper to compute (no logarithm). scikit-learn defaults to Gini for `DecisionTreeClassifier`.

For **regression trees**, impurity is typically **variance** (MSE) of y in the node — split to minimize weighted variance of child nodes.

### Prediction path

At inference, start at the **root**, follow left/right branches according to feature tests, stop at a **leaf**, return its stored prediction (class or numeric value). Complexity is O(depth), not O(number of training samples).

### Complexity knobs

Tree **capacity** is controlled by hyperparameters (see **Model Complexity**):

- **max_depth** — maximum path length from root to leaf.
- **min_samples_split** — minimum samples required to attempt a split.
- **min_samples_leaf** — minimum samples per leaf (prevents tiny, high-variance leaves).
- **max_leaf_nodes**, **min_impurity_decrease** — additional pruning-style controls.

A **deep, unconstrained** tree can memorize training noise (**high variance**). A **stump** (depth 1) is a weak, high-bias model.

### Axis-aligned rules and feature types

CART uses **axis-aligned** thresholds: one feature at a time. **Numeric** features split at real thresholds; **categorical** features are handled via **ordered encodings** or **one-hot** columns (see **Encoding**). High-cardinality raw strings need encoding before standard CART implementations can split on them.

### Classification vs regression trees

Same tree structure, different leaf values and impurity:

- **Classifier**: Gini/entropy impurity, majority vote at leaves, metrics like accuracy or log loss on held-out data.
- **Regressor**: variance/MSE impurity, mean prediction at leaves, metrics like RMSE or MAE.

**Regression vs Classification** defines the problem type; this topic owns the **tree algorithm** for both.

### Relationship to ensembles

A single tree is interpretable but unstable. **Random forests** and **gradient boosting** combine many trees — they inherit split mechanics from CART but add bagging, feature subsampling, or sequential correction. Learn single-tree CART here first; ensembles build on it.

### Key takeaway

Decision trees turn supervised learning into a sequence of local, interpretable rules. CART grows them by greedy impurity minimization; Gini and entropy are the canonical classification impurity measures; depth and leaf constraints trade bias against variance.
keyPoints: CART grows binary, axis-aligned trees greedily by maximizing impurity reduction at each split.
Classification leaves predict majority class; regression leaves predict mean target.
Gini impurity and entropy are the canonical classification impurity measures — defined here.
max_depth, min_samples_leaf, and related hyperparameters control model complexity and overfitting.
Single trees are interpretable but high-variance; random forests and boosting combine many trees.
